data.title: Mercado Libre: Growth Flywheel In Place - Ignore Near-Term Noise
data.description:
I previously rated MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI) as a Buy in November 2025, thanks to the accelerated growth prospects across its core offerings.

In this article, I shall discuss why MELI offers a great dip buying opportunity after the selloff, with their intensified logistics/fintech investments likely to be accretive to its diversified commercial/advertising growth flywheel.

MELI Faces Numerous Headwinds & Tailwinds Entering FY2026

Since my last Buy rating, MELI has went ahead and breached its uptrend support line established since the June 2022 bottom, to hit a new 52 week lows of $1.59K, with a similar underperformance also observed in its diversified commerce/logistics/fintech peers.

For MELI, the recent meltdown may also be attributed to Brazil's potentially higher inflationary pressure from the developing Iran conflict, since the country comprises 52.6% of its revenues in FY2025.

With MELI set to report their FQ1'26 earnings call on May 07, 2026, I urge readers to observe their upcoming performance metrics, since it remains to be seen if their profit margins may further deteriorate from prior levels, across:

  • the operating margins of 10.1% in FQ4'25 (-3.4 points YoY) and
  • the net income margins of 6.4% (-4.1 points YoY).

With the management not offering any forward guidance, I can understand why the market has de-risked first as observed in the stock's steep correction by -39% between the 52 week highs and lows.

For now, readers may want to note that much of MELI's COGS headwinds are attributed to the logistics segment's new fulfillment centers (including free shipping, 1P, cross-border) and the fintech segment's new credit card offerings.

The market's fears may be somewhat warranted, since these initiatives have contributed to a margin compression by "5 to 6 percentage point" in FQ4'25, with the management's commentary of them "not hesitate to invest and to order to capture those opportunities as we have done in the past, even if that puts some short-term margin pressure" implying further margin headwinds in the intermediate term.

These reasons are also why I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the FQ1'26 earnings call, since MELI's bottom-lines may disappoint before the management is able to drive operating leverage for the logistics segment and improved funding costs for the fintech segment.

1. Logistics Investment Drives Improved Commerce Engagement

For one, MELI's growth initiatives across the logistics/fintech segments have already contributed to the commerce segment's growth flywheel, as observed in:

  • the growing active unique buyers at 83.2M in FQ4'25 (+23.6% YoY) with a impressive net adds of 15.9M YoY (+23.2% YoY), and
  • the accelerating items sold growth by +43% YoY against a year ago at +27% YoY,
  • as they ramp up Same & Next Day Shipments by +29% YoY against a year ago at +21% YoY.

These successes naturally underscore MELI's excellent value proposition across commerce/logistics, with the management's decision to lower the free shipping threshold also "bringing new buyers into the ecosystem" and "driving higher purchase frequency" by +15% YoY against a year ago at +3% YoY.

With the increase in shipping volumes already driving productivity gains, I am of the opinion that the company's near-term margin pains are unlikely to last once they achieve improved operating leverage from the higher commerce/shipping volumes.

If anything, I have observed a similar trend in other players, including Amazon (AMZN) and Sea Limited (SE), with their vertically integrated commerce and fulfillment/logistics capabilities allowing them to drive durable and incremental monetization trends.

This is especially since MELI has "a selection of international products available, primarily from sellers in China and the U.S., through our cross-border trade (“CBT”) operations."

While CBT sales from “Other Countries (including Bermuda, Chile, China, Colombia, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Peru, Uruguay and the U.S.)” remain a small part of MELI’s net revenues at 4.4% in FY2025, the expanded “fulfillment, cross docking and service centers” in these countries may allow them to tap into China’s e-commerce market size of $2T and the US at $1.22T in 2026 to drive further growth opportunities, against the Latam e-commerce market size of $215B.

As a result, I am of the opinion that MELI's recent bottom-line pains are likely to be temporary, as previously observed with AMZN in FY2021/FY2022.

2. Fintech Penetration

As for MELI's new credit card offerings, it goes without saying that adoption trends have been rather robust, as observed in:

  • the higher consumer loans at $4.5B (+75.7% YoY), and
  • the expanding credit card portfolio of approximately $5.62B in FQ4'25 (+114.5% YoY), based on the 45% of the $12.5B in AUM.

This is on top of the higher Assets Under Management [AUM] at $18.81B (+78% YoY), as more of its users stay within its commerce/fintech ecosystem (digital banking/savings/investments), as discussed above.

Particularly, readers may want to note that the higher AUM already contributes to MELI's lower funding costs on a QoQ basis in FQ4'25, with a further moderation likely over the next few quarters.

These reasons may also be why eMarketer already expects "30% of every $10 spent online in the region will occur on Mercado Libre’s digital marketplace" by 2026, "up from slightly less than a quarter in 2022," with the logistics/fintech investments efforts expected to drive the robust growth prospects and market share gains in the commerce segment.

A similar approach has also been observed in SE's leading commerce platform and their growing focus in the fintech segment across lending/BNPL (SPayLater), which have contributed to Shopee's expanding regional commerce share to 52% in 2024 (+4 points YoY).

Therefore, while MELI may not offer a forward guidance, their accelerated revenue growth by +44.6% in FQ4'25 against +37.5% YoY in FQ4'24 has been extremely impressive indeed, with it perhaps contributing to the raised consensus revenue growth estimates at a CAGR of +25.7% through FY2028.

Inversely, their decelerating EPS growth profile at -12.5% YoY in FQ4'25 and +4.5% YoY in FY2025, against the 4Y historical profile at +120.4% (the period of time that they are EPS profitable), have contributed to the consistently downgraded consensus EPS estimates, albeit still somewhat decent at a CAGR of +34.8% through FY2028.

3. Near-Term Risks

For now, I urge readers to monitor MELI's balance sheet health in the upcoming FQ1'26 earnings call, attributed to:

  • the deteriorating net debt level of $4.68B in FY2025 (-108% YoY) and
  • the elevated net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of 1.16x (compared to a year ago at 0.69x),

despite the richer LTM adj EBITDA by +23.7%.

When comparing to its diversified commerce peers, including:

  • AMZN at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of -0.39x in FY2025,
  • SE at -2.54x in FY2025, and
  • Alibaba Group (BABA) at -4.11x in FQ3'26,

it is apparent that MELI's deteriorating balance sheet may be a cause for concern, with it signaling their overly fast and furious investment cadence while explaining the market's recent de-risking.

While the announcement of a ceasefire in Iran has contributed to the market-wide optimism, readers may want to note that MELI may suffer temporarily impacted H1'26 performance metrics.

This is because Brazil is already experiencing a higher annual inflation rate of 4.14% in March 2026, "further above the central bank’s 3% target." (original source from Bloomberg in Portuguese language, translated version from MSN in English language).

If the inflationary pressure continues to rise, MELI may face impacted growth prospects, since the company may not replicate their prior growth acceleration in Brazil, where "GMV grew an impressive 35% year-over-year alongside a 45% increase in sold items" in FQ4'25.

This is on top of MELI's potentially impacted fintech segment, since more Brazilian families are facing structural debt issues, with the higher inflationary pressure potentially triggering further downward pressure on the fintech segment's:

  • Net Interest Margin After Losses [NIMAL], down from the FQ4'25 levels of 23.3% (-4.3 points YoY), and
  • Non Performing Loans [NPL], up from the FQ4'25 levels of 7.6% for the 15-90 days past due) and 16.8% for the > 90 days past due.

As a result of these risks, I urge readers to temper their expectations entering the FQ1'26 earnings call, since their performance metrics may temporarily disappoint before eventually improving from H2'26 onwards, assuming a swift resolution in Iran.

4. Meltdown Triggers Improved Entry Points

With the steep price correction by -39.7% between the 52 week highs and lows already mitigating the bottom-line headwinds, I am of the opinion that MELI is still compelling at FWD EV/Sales valuation of 2.59x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 37.02x.

This is down from the recent highs of 4.59x/49.83x and the 5Y mean of 5.73x/2,198.47x, respectively.

Particularly, despite the decelerating nature of its EPS growth prospects at a 3Y CAGR of +34.8%, MELI is still attractively valued at my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.06x, based on the FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 37.02x.

When comparing MELI's FWD EV/Sales values of 2.59x, the consensus revenue growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +25.7%, and my estimated 3Y PEG non-GAAP ratio of 1.06x to its diversified commerce peers, including:

  • global/North American focused - AMZN at 3.39x/+12.3%/1.77x,
  • Southeast Asia focused - SE at 1.60x/+21.6%/0.92x, and
  • global/China focused - BABA at 2.08x/+8.5%/10.2x, respectively,

it is apparent that MELI's double digits, top/bottom-line growth prospects remain reasonable valued here, thanks to the steep correction unlocking an improved entry point.

This is especially since MELI has been deploying AI and agentic capabilities to drive improved advertising ROI and search targeting, with their new AI seller assistant also driving higher live listing, reduced lead times, and improved customer support results.

Given the relatively small ad penetration against its growing GMV, I am of the opinion that the advertising segment remains a nascent opportunity, with it potentially being a growth driver given the typically high margin advertising business.

The same has been similarly observed with AMZN recently joining the Big 2 advertising giants, namely Google (GOOG) and Meta (META), to form an advertising oligopoly.

MELI Remains A Great Buy Here

For now, MELI has seemingly found a floor after the prior H2'25 meltdown at the $1.59Ks, aided by the rather bullish recovery by +16.9% since then.

A similar development has also been observed in MELI's bottoming technical indicators by the end of March 2026 and the consequent V shaped recovery by the time of writing, with the worst of the profit margin pessimism already baked in.

This is especially since the meltdown has contributed to the expanded upside potential of +58.7% to my base-case long-term price target of $2.89K, based on the FY2028 EPS performance of $96.48 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +34.8%) and the recent March 2026 P/E bottom of 30x (near to its peer group P/E mean of 30x).

As a result of the profitable growth trends, the growing engagement across its well diversified commerce/logistics/fintech/advertising offerings, the cheaper valuations nearer to its peers, and the technical bottoming, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the MELI stock here.

Do not miss this dollar cost averaging opportunity.
